pattern: Zeroing the array only ever needs a MINIMAL set of kept queries, and at every position the best query to keep in reserve is always the one that reaches furthest -- a sweep with a max-heap of candidates finds that minimal set directly.
hints: Reframe the goal: instead of maximizing how many queries you remove, find the smallest possible set of queries you must keep so every index is still covered enough times.

Sweep left to right, and whenever the current index isn't yet covered enough by already-kept queries, you must keep one more -- and among the available candidates that start at or before this index, keeping the one whose range reaches furthest is never worse than keeping any other.
If you ever need one more kept query but none of the candidates that start early enough still reach the current index, zeroing the array is impossible no matter which queries you pick.

edge_inputs.description: statement example, impossible even keeping everything
single element exactly coverable by its only query
all queries identical and fully redundant
one query barely reaches an index needing 2 units

stress.description: random small demands with random covering ranges
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 1500))
    nums = [rng.randint(0, 4) for _ in range(n)]
    m = rng.randint(1, 120)
    queries = []
    for _ in range(m):
        l = rng.randint(0, n - 1)
        r = rng.randint(l, n - 1)
        queries.append([l, r])
    return [nums, queries]
